figures.caption: Table A.5: Multi-seed quantitative results across benchmarks. We repeat each experiment with five independent training seeds {42, 200, 500, 2010, 2026}. The generated datasets and evaluation samples are fixed across seeds, while model initialization and training stochasticity are varied. We report mean ± standard deviation for relative L2 error and Barron norm relative error. Bold values indicate the best mean performance for each metric.

Figure A.6: In-distribution input-resolution analysis of UFO on the 2D Burgers equation. UFO is trained with 100× 100 input observations and evaluated at λ = 4.5 using progressively coarser input resolutions from 100× 100 to 55× 55. The output query resolution is fixed. Unlike the OOD setting in Fig. 5, where UFO remains largely stable under input sparsification, the in-distribution error increases smoothly as input observations become coarser, reflecting the benefit of dense input information for fine-grained ID reconstruction.
Figure 4: Comparison on GRF-Helmholtz under OOD correlation lengths with moderate wave number k = 60 in the top panel and extreme wave number k = 120 in the bottom panel.

Figure A.10: Temporal profile visualization on StepHeat. Predictions are shown for UFO, at s = 0.32, 0.41, 0.52. Each subplot compares the predicted and reference one-dimensional solution profiles at multiple time snapshots t ∈ {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9}.
Figure A.9: In-distribution predictions on the parametric 2D Burgers equation using FNO.
Figure A.13: Additional δ-Helmholtz visualizations of UFO with colorbars and absolute error maps in the cases corresponding to Fig. 2.
Table 1: ID performance on StepHeat with varying discontinuity location s. Relative L2 and Barron errors are computed over the full spatio-temporal solution field, lower is better.
Figure A.14: Additional δ-Helmholtz visualizations of DeepONet with colorbars and absolute error maps in the cases corresponding to Fig. 2.

Table A.3: Trainable parameters and per-epoch runtime across benchmarks. UFO uses fewer than 8 × 104 parameters in all settings and maintains competitive runtime, while DeepONet and FNO often require larger parameter counts or higher per-epoch cost.
Figure A.8: In-distribution predictions on the parametric 2D Burgers equation using DeepONet.

Figure 3: Structural comparison on the parametric 2D Burgers equation under bidirectional OOD generalization. Models are trained on λ ∈ [3, 6]. A discretized colormap is used to reveal topological inconsistencies more clearly, where regions belonging to the same value range may become fragmented or disconnected.
Figure A.15: Additional δ-Helmholtz visualizations of FNO with colorbars and absolute error maps in the cases corresponding to Fig. 2.

Figure 5: Resolution behavior of UFO on Burgers. Top row: relative L2 and Barron errors under decreasing input resolutions for different λ values. Second row: output query resolution study at λ = 5.8.

Figure A.12: Temporal profile visualization on StepHeat. Predictions are shown for FNO, at s = 0.32, 0.41, 0.52. Each subplot compares the predicted and reference one-dimensional solution profiles at multiple time snapshots t ∈ {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9}.

Figure 2: Qualitative comparison on δ-Helmholtz for interpolation and extrapolation of global shifts. The model is trained on δ ∈ [−5, 5] with 256 samples in total. δ = 4.3 is interpolation, while δ = ±30.8 are strong extrapolation cases. Each UFO sample is observed on a randomly non-uniform input discretization.
Figure A.7: In-distribution predictions on the parametric 2D Burgers equation using UFO.

Table A.4: Summary of benchmarks used in Section 3. Each benchmark is designed to probe the operator realization from distinct views, including spectral bias, irregular input sampling, nonlinear structure preservation, stochastic high-frequency fields, and discretization behavior.

Figure A.16: Multiple test samples under OOD correlation lengths ℓ = 0.05 and ℓ = 0.35, with relative L2 and Barron errors reported below each prediction. The top panel presents the predictions at k = 60, while the bottom panel shows the results at k = 120.

Figure A.11: Temporal profile visualization on StepHeat. Predictions are shown for DeepONet, at s = 0.32, 0.41, 0.52. Each subplot compares the predicted and reference one-dimensional solution profiles at multiple time snapshots t ∈ {0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9}.
Figure 1: Architecture of the UFO framework.
